preamble: The transformation from a voiceless uvular plosive /q/ to a voiced postalveolar affricate /d͡ʒ/ represents a significant phonetic shift involving changes in place, manner, and voicing. This complex sound change is primarily observed as a result of palatalization, often with an intermediate stage where the uvular plosive first voices and fronts to a velar stop before undergoing affrication and further fronting to the postalveolar region.
phoneticEffects: Fronting (uvular to postalveolar), Voicing (voiceless to voiced), Affrication (plosive to affricate), Palatalization

examples.note: Dialectal realization of the letter Qāf (ق)
Dialectal realization of the letter Qāf (ق), sometimes as a palatalized allophone of /g/

sources: Al-Salih, S. (1989). Dirāsāt fī Fiqh al-Lughah. Beirut: Dār al-ʻIlm lil-Malāyīn.
Anis, I. (1995). Fī al-Lahajāt al-ʻArabīyah. Cairo: Maktabat al-Anglo al-Miṣrīyah.
Mustafawi, A. (2006). Lenition of /dʒ/ to /j/ in Qatari Arabic: An Optimality Theory Approach. Qatar University.
Johnstone, T. M. (1967). Eastern Arabian Dialect Studies. London: Oxford University Press.
